sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 5 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 27, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 26 of about 78 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: The agent didn't move - only 1 cell changed at [63, 51], which is likely the timer bar. The agent is still at rows 8-12, cols 38-42. Let me check the agent's position.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check agent position
seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] == 'b' and n['pixels'] > 5:
        print(f"id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Check maze below agent
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("\nRows 12-22, cols 35-46:")
for i in range(12, 23):
    row = ""
    for j in range(35, 47):
        ch = ascii_lines[i][j]
        if ch == 'W':
            row += '.'
        elif ch == 'B':
            row += '#'
        elif ch == 'R':
            row += 'R'
        elif ch == 'b':
            row += '@'
        else:
            row += '?'
    print(f"Row {i:2d}: {row}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The agent is still at rows 8-12, cols 38-42. The DOWN action didn't move the agent. Looking at the maze:
- Rows 12-13: ###@@@@@#... and #####R###...
- Rows 14-22: ....#R#.....
At rows 14-18, cols 38-42:
Row 14: cols 38-42 are #R# (but the ascii shows ....#R#.....)
Wait, let me re-examine. The ascii shows:
Row 14: ....#R#.....
Cols 35-38: .... (white)
Cols 39-41: #R# (black wall with red path)
Cols 42-46: ..... (white)
So at rows 14-18, cols 38-42 includes white cells at cols 38 and 42. The agent needs a 5x5 area of black cells, so it can't move to rows 14-18, cols 38-42.
But wait, the agent is at rows 8-12, cols 38-42. Looking at the ascii:
Row 8: ###@@@@@#...
Cols 35-37: ### (black wall)
Cols 38-42: @@@@@ (agent)
Cols 43-46: #... (black wall and white)
So the agent is at cols 38-42, which is within the black area. The agent can't move DOWN because rows 14-18, cols 38-42 includes white cells.
Looking at the boundary of the black area:
[[7, 13], [7, 43], [13, 43], [13, 42], [14, 41], [41, 41], ...]
At rows 14+, the black area is only at col 41 (the right edge). So the agent can't move DOWN from rows 8-12, cols 38-42 because the black area narrows to just col 41.
The agent needs to move LEFT to reach a wider part of the black area, then move DOWN.
Looking at the maze:
- Rows 8-12, cols 32-36: part of the black area
- Rows 8-12, cols 26-30: part of the black area
- Rows 8-12, cols 20-24: part of the black area
- Rows 8-12, cols 14-18: part of the black area
From rows 8-12, the agent can move LEFT to cols 32-36, then DOWN to explore.
But wait, looking at the ascii at rows 14-18:
Row 14: #######.....#######.......#R#
Cols 13-19: ####### (black wall)
Cols 20-24: ..... (white passage)
Cols 25-31: ####### (black wall)
Cols 32-38: ....... (white passage)
Cols 39-41: #R# (black wall with red path)
At rows 14-18, the black corridors are at cols 13-19, cols 25-31, and cols 39-41. The agent is at rows 8-12, cols 38-42. Moving LEFT would take the agent to cols 32-36, but at rows 14-18, cols 32-36 is a white passage.
So the agent needs to move LEFT to cols 26-30, then DOWN to rows 14-18, cols 26-30 (which is part of the black wall at cols 25-31).
Let me try moving LEFT twice to reach cols 26-30, then DOWN.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Move LEFT twice to reach cols 26-30, then DOWN
action(['LEFT', 'LEFT', 'DOWN'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
